title: Quiz: Operators in Quantum Mechanics
description: Test your understanding of Hermitian, unitary, and projection operators

questions.question: Which property must an operator have to represent a physical observable?
questions.explanation: Hermitian operators (A = A†) represent physical observables because they have real eigenvalues, which correspond to real measurement outcomes.

questions.question: What is the defining property of a unitary operator U?
questions.explanation: A unitary operator satisfies U†U = UU† = I. This means U† = U⁻¹.

questions.question: All quantum gates must be unitary operators.
questions.explanation: True! Unitarity ensures quantum evolution is reversible and preserves the norm of quantum states (total probability = 1).

questions.question: What are the possible eigenvalues of a projection operator?
questions.explanation: Projectors satisfy P² = P. If Pv = λv, then P²v = λ²v = Pv = λv, so λ² = λ, giving λ = 0 or λ = 1.

questions.question: If H is Hermitian, what type of operator is e^{iH}?
questions.explanation: For Hermitian H, (e^{iH})† = e^{-iH} = (e^{iH})⁻¹, so e^{iH} is unitary. This is how quantum gates are constructed from Hamiltonians.

questions.question: A Hermitian operator always has real eigenvalues.
questions.explanation: True! This is a fundamental property of Hermitian operators and is why they can represent physical observables (measurements yield real values).

questions.question: If U is unitary, what can we say about its eigenvalues λ?
questions.explanation: Unitary operators have eigenvalues on the unit circle: |λ| = 1. They can be written as λ = e^{iθ} for some real θ.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about the Pauli X matrix? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: All are true! X is both Hermitian (X = X†) and unitary (X†X = I). Its eigenvalues are +1 and -1, and X² = I (it's its own inverse).
